danger_signals.visual_form: Masses of enemies filling dungeon spaces
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat zone; player must engage or evade

danger_signals.visual_form: Health bar constantly decreasing as game progresses
danger_signals.meaning: Ambient environmental danger; survival pressure increases over time
Progression blocked; must defeat enemies or find key to advance

danger_signals.visual_form: Unique enemy entities in Quest mode dungeons
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat; progression gate requiring significant challenge overcome

safety_signals.meaning: Health replenishment; safe moment to recover
Screen-clear ability; temporary invulnerability or massive threat reduction
Escape routes; player agency in choosing difficulty/direction
Temporary attribute boost; tactical advantage window

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four different playable character classes (Archer, Warrior, Wizard, Valkyrie)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player projectiles/visual effects distinguish from enemy attacks

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Masses of varied enemy types within each dungeon
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Hostile projectile patterns, distinct sprite sheets from player characters

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sequential dungeon layouts with increasing visual complexity; new enemy types introduced per stage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy density and aggression scales; health drain accelerates; boss encounters trigger at stage completion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Health bar visibly depleted; urgent UI state
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Must seek food items; enemy threat becomes overwhelming if health not restored

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Quest mode progression with character leveling
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character stats increase; visual feedback from level-up system
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player power escalation allows handling stronger enemy groups; RPG progression unlocks new tactical options

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enclosed arena space; hidden power-up spawns at specific locations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player vs. player combat; projectile bouncing mechanic enables ring-out victories; attribute bonuses create temporary power advantage

environmental_storytelling.example: Multiple exits per stage signal non-linear world; different paths lead to different dungeons, implying a large interconnected underground realm
New enemy variants in each Quest mode dungeon establish thematic coherence; visual monster design changes suggest different dungeon regions/purposes
Environmental attrition (health loss over time) communicates the dungeon itself as hostile; player must move, not rest
Strategic distribution of food/potions signals safe pockets within dangerous spaces; visual landmarks become resource nodes

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Projectile weapon fire at enemies; collision with food items triggers health recovery; exit traversal advances progression
Urgent need to seek consumables; movement pattern may become evasive rather than aggressive
Projectile attacks toward player; blocking exits; forcing player movement and engagement
Distinct attack patterns; higher durability; defeat triggers level/stage completion and progression reward
Enhanced attributes (attack, defense, speed); projectile bouncing mechanic enables ring-out tactics

notes: Gauntlet IV uses classical dungeon-crawler visual language: enemy density as danger metric, health as core resource pressure, and multi-exit design to communicate player agency. The four game modes (Arcade, Quest, Battle, Record) create distinct world-state contexts with varying visual/behavioral feedback. Quest mode's RPG leveling system introduces progression fantasy via stat escalation. Battle mode transforms the engine into competitive space where projectile physics (bouncing) and power-up placement become primary world-state communicators. The constant health drain is a key design choice that keeps the dungeon environment actively hostile even without enemies present. Character class variety (Archer/Warrior/Wizard/Valkyrie) implies faction identity through gameplay role, though visual faction markers are minimal in available documentation.
